stops.name: Gwanghwamun Gate
stops.shortDescription: The magnificent main and largest gate of Gyeongbokgung Palace. Guarded by colorful royal sentries, it serves as the grand historical entrance against the backdrop of modern Seoul and Mount Bugaksan.

stops.name: Gyeongbokgung Palace
stops.shortDescription: Built in 1395, this was the primary and most beautiful royal palace of the Joseon Dynasty. Its vast, tranquil grounds include grand throne halls, elegant lotus ponds, and ancient pavilions.

stops.name: Samcheong-dong Street
stops.shortDescription: A charming, tree-lined street winding just east of the palace. It perfectly blends traditional architecture with chic modern cafes, artisan boutiques, and contemporary art galleries.

stops.name: Bukchon Hanok Village
stops.shortDescription: A wonderfully preserved historic neighborhood featuring hundreds of traditional Korean houses (hanoks) with gracefully curved wooden roofs. It offers stunning hilltop views over the modern city below.

stops.name: Changdeokgung Palace
stops.shortDescription: A UNESCO World Heritage site known for its exceptional harmony with the natural topography. It is most famous for its 'Secret Garden' (Huwon), a vast ecological oasis originally reserved for the king.

stops.name: Insadong (Ssamzigil)
stops.shortDescription: The focal point of Korean traditional culture and crafts. The main street is packed with antique shops, traditional teahouses, and Ssamzigil, a unique spiraling shopping complex.

stops.name: Jogyesa Temple
stops.shortDescription: The chief temple of the Jogye Order of Korean Buddhism. Despite being located in the middle of a bustling metropolis, it remains a serene sanctuary featuring centuries-old locust and baeksong trees.

stops.name: Tapgol Park
stops.shortDescription: Seoul's first modern park and the historical epicenter of the 1919 March 1st Movement for Korean independence. It houses the intricate, 10-tier Wongaksa Pagoda enclosed in glass.

stops.name: Bosingak Pavilion
stops.shortDescription: A large belfry housing a massive historic bronze bell. During the Joseon Dynasty, the bell was rung exactly 33 times every morning to open the city gates and 28 times at night to close them.

stops.name: Cheonggyecheon Stream
stops.shortDescription: A massive urban renewal project that uncovered a hidden historical stream. It is now a 10-kilometer sunken, landscaped pedestrian haven completely isolated from the traffic noise above.

stops.name: Myeongdong Shopping Street
stops.shortDescription: One of Seoul's main shopping and tourism districts, famed worldwide for its countless K-beauty cosmetic stores and an incredible, chaotic array of delicious street food stalls.

stops.name: Myeongdong Cathedral
stops.shortDescription: The birthplace of the Roman Catholic Church community in Korea. Built in 1898, this beautiful Neo-Gothic brick cathedral served as a critical refuge for pro-democracy activists in the 1980s.

stops.name: Seoul Plaza & City Hall
stops.shortDescription: A vast elliptical grassy plaza fronting both the historic 1920s stone library and the striking, wave-shaped glass architecture of the modern Seoul City Hall.

stops.name: Deoksugung Palace
stops.shortDescription: A highly unique royal palace blending traditional Korean timber-frame buildings with Western-style neoclassical stone architecture. Its beautifully lit stone-wall road is famously romantic for evening strolls.
